title: Dog Ate Grapes? What to Do Immediately - Vet Triage Guide
meta_desc: Dog ate grapes or raisins? Learn why this is a serious emergency, what symptoms to watch for, and why immediate veterinary attention is crucial.
content_html:
TL;DR: Grapes and raisins are highly toxic to dogs and can cause sudden kidney failure. Contact a veterinarian or animal poison control immediately, regardless of the amount ingested.

What should I do if my dog ate grapes or raisins?

  • Ingesting grapes or raisins is extremely dangerous for dogs and can lead to acute kidney failure.
  • Even a small amount can be toxic, as the exact toxic substance and mechanism are not yet fully understood.
  • It is vital to act immediately to protect your pet's health.

Is my dog eating grapes or raisins a veterinary emergency?

  • This situation is considered a high-urgency veterinary emergency.
  • The severity of toxicity varies between dogs, and it is impossible to predict which individuals will be severely affected.
  • Prompt veterinary intervention is the best course of action to prevent dire consequences like kidney failure.

How does taking a photo of what my dog ate help with triage?

  • A photo assists veterinary staff in identifying the specific item your dog ingested, such as distinguishing grapes from other objects.
  • It provides a visual reference of your dog's general condition and any immediate symptoms.
  • Do not delay seeking professional help to take a photo; your dog's immediate health is the priority.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Following significant grape or raisin ingestion in dogs, prompt decontamination is recommended. If the ingestion was recent and exceeds 1-2 g/kg, emesis should be induced with 3% hydrogen peroxide (2 mL/kg, not exceeding 45 mL), followed by activated charcoal, especially with large ingestions. In cases of large ingestions, or when spontaneous vomiting and/or diarrhea develop within 12 hours, aggressive fluid diuresis for a minimum of 48 hours is recommended, monitoring renal function and fluid balance. For oliguric patients, dopamine (0.5-3 mcg/kg/min, IV) and/or furosemide (2 mg/kg, IV) may be considered to stimulate urine production. Anuric patients are unlikely to survive without peritoneal dialysis or hemodialysis, and even then, the prognosis is guarded.

Chapter: Toxicology, Nephrology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 2968)

faqs.question: What happens if my dog eats grapes?
faqs.answer: Grapes and raisins are highly toxic to dogs and can cause sudden, acute kidney failure. This can occur even with small ingestions.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of grape poisoning in dogs?
faqs.answer: Symptoms may include vomiting, diarrhea, lethargy, loss of appetite, abdominal pain, dehydration, and decreased urination or no urination at all (indicating kidney failure).

faqs.question: How quickly do symptoms appear?
faqs.answer: Symptoms can appear within a few hours, but kidney damage may begin to occur even before visible signs. This is why immediate veterinary care is crucial.

faqs.question: What should I do if my dog ate grapes?
faqs.answer: Contact your veterinarian or an emergency animal hospital IMMEDIATELY. Do not wait for symptoms to appear. Your vet may advise you to induce vomiting or bring your dog in right away for treatment.

faqs.question: Is there an antidote for grape poisoning?
faqs.answer: There is no specific antidote for grape toxicity. Treatment focuses on decontamination (inducing vomiting if recent ingestion) and supportive care to protect the kidneys.
